summary: Cantrill humorously critiques the launch of Cuil, a hyped Google competitor from 2008, by pointing out the absurd image associations their search engine returned for 'dtrace' queries. Despite his stated policy of not publicly mocking others' software bugs, he makes an exception for Cuil's comically wrong results, which included images of quilts, strip malls, and the Palisades Interstate Parkway. The post serves as a gentle takedown of overhyped technology that fails to deliver on basic functionality.
key_points: Cuil was heavily hyped as a potential Google competitor in 2008
The search engine returned bizarre, unrelated images alongside search results
Even basic searches like 'dtrace' produced comically wrong visual associations
The post demonstrates how overhyped technology often fails at fundamentals
money_quotes: In general, I don't believe in drawing attention to bugs in the software of others: any significant body of software is likely to have bugs, and I think one can too easily draw overly broad inferences by looking at software through the lens of its defects.
When a new search engine pops up, my egotistical reflex is to first search for 'dtrace'.

I can't say for certain that I've never travelled on the Palisades Interstate Parkway, but with all deference to that 38.25-mile stretch of tarmac, I do believe that I can say that it played no role in DTrace – or DTrace in it.
key_insight: Overhyped technology competitors often fail spectacularly at basic functionality, and sometimes the bugs are so absurd they warrant breaking one's own rule against public criticism.
